title: Business Scenarios — an Essence Practice Extension
kicker: A composable practice · plugs into the Kernel + MVA/MVG deck
lede: The TOGAF Business Scenario technique, re-expressed as a small, composable Essence practice. It adds almost no new structure — instead it becomes the engine that advances alphas you already have, sized the MVA way: one living document, not a wall of
text: Business Scenarios — an Essence Practice Extension Skip to main content Show All Alphas Activities Work Product Roles Patterns ⎙ Print This practice is a synthesis: it expresses the Business Scenario method in Essence terms and maps it onto the existing Kernel + MVA/MVG deck. It paraphrases the technique rather than reproducing The Open Group guide — keep your licensed copy of the TOGAF® Series Guide: Business Scenarios as the authoritative source for the method itself. "Drives the alpha" notation shows which alpha state each element pushes toward.

title: An Essence Way of Working — Introduction
kicker: Start here · the composed way of working
lede: This is the front door to a complete way of working built on the OMG / SEMAT Essence standard: a kernel of universal things to track, plus four composable practices that move them. This page explains Essence, then shows how the kernel and practices f
text: An Essence Way of Working — Introduction Skip to main content Jump to What is Essence The language The kernel Levels of scale The practices Way of working Moving the alphas ⎙ Print Essence is an Open Group / OMG standard developed by the SEMAT community (Ivar Jacobson and others). This way of working composes the Essence kernel with four practices — Business Scenarios and Service Blueprint (discovery & service design), Minimum Viable Architecture and Minimum Viable Governance — synthesising ideas from TOGAF, the TOGAF Business Scenarios method, service blueprinting (Shostack, 1984) and Use-Case 2.0. The composition and card form are a synthesis for learning; keep your licensed standards as the authority.

sections.h: Levels of scale — naming the System

title: The Essence Kernel
lede: The universal scaffolding every software endeavour shares: seven Alphas, fifteen Activity Spaces and six Competencies, across three areas of concern. Practices — Business Scenarios, MVA, MVG and your delivery practices — plug in on top. The kernel it
text: The Essence Kernel Skip to main content Show All Alphas Activity Spaces Competencies ⎙ Print This is the kernel only — it tells you what to track and that work must be done, not how . The “how” comes from practices composed on top. Kernel content follows the OMG Essence specification; descriptions are paraphrased to fit the card.

title: Essence — Training
lede: A training in the kernel, the four practices and the assessment workbook — how to use them together to move the alphas.
text: Essence — Training Skip to main content Essence training Essence a practical way of working A training in the kernel, the four practices and the assessment workbook — how to use them together to move the alphas. Use → ← arrows to navigate · F11 for fullscreen Welcome · Agenda What we'll cover today Six sections — from the standard, through the practices, to the workbook that measures progress. 01 Foundations What Essence is, the language of element types, the kernel and the alpha state mechanic. 02 Levels of scale The System is the system of interest — and it is fractal. Pick the level before anything else. 03 The four practices Business Scenarios, Service Blueprint, MVA and MVG — what each does and the alphas it moves. 04 Composing the way Stacking the practices on the kernel and running them in a sensible order — including a decision guide. 05 The assessment workbook Measuring progress by alpha state — how to use the dashboard and tabs in practice. 06 Wrap-up Key takeaways and pointers to the full reference decks. Section 01 Foundations The Essence standard, in a handful of ideas. Foundations What is Essence — and why use it? What it is An OMG standard from the SEMAT initiative (Ivar Jacobson and others) that captures the common ground of any software endeavour as a thinking framework , not a fixed method. You get a small language , a kernel of universals every endeavour shares, and practices that plug in to form a complete method. Why use it Methodology fatigue is real. Big-process projects often produce big documents and small progress. Essence flips this: progress is judged by the health of essential things — alphas — not by the documents you wrote. It is lightweight, composable, and travels with you across teams and projects. The core move: compose your way of working from practices, and judge progress by alpha state. Foundations · Element types Six element types — the language Everything in Essence — kernel and practices — is built from these six. Colours are consistent across every deck in this set. Alpha Things you progress An essential thing whose health you track — it has ordered states . Activity Space What must be done A space for work the endeavour cannot skip; concrete activities fill it. Competency Abilities people bring Held at five levels: Assists → Applies → Masters → Adapts → Innovates. Work Product Tangible artifacts Evidence of an alpha's progress — a document, model, repo, decision record. Role Patterns of who does the work Hats people wear, not job titles. One person can wear several. Pattern Reusable rules of thumb Biases that shape every decision a practice makes. Foundations · Areas of concern Three areas of concern Every alpha lives in one of these — the customer you serve, the solution you build, the endeavour that does the work. Customer · Opportunity · Stakeholders Solution · Requirements · System · Architecture · Architecture Decisions · Architectural Drivers Endeavor · Work · Team · Way of Working · Governance Fou

title: Essence Way of Working — Home
lede: One kernel of universal things to track, four composable practices that move them. Start with the introduction, explore the kernel, then work through each practice in the logical order below.
text: Essence Way of Working — Home Skip to main content Jump to Start here Practices How they compose Workbook 01 Start here New to Essence? Read the introduction first, then skim the kernel reference to understand what the alphas are and how state progressions work. Introduction An Essence Way of Working The front door — what Essence is, the six element types, the kernel, how practices compose, and the logical order in which they move the alphas. Covers → kernel + all four practices Open introduction → Foundation The Essence Kernel 7 alphas, 15 activity spaces, 6 competencies — the universal scaffolding every software endeavour shares, across three areas of concern. Provides → the alphas every practice advances Open the kernel deck → Training Essence — Training A full training in the kernel, the four practices, and the assessment workbook — how to use them together to move the alphas. Format → slide-style walkthrough Open training → 02 The four practices Each practice is a composable unit that plugs into the kernel and advances specific alphas. Run them in the order below — they form a loop that nests at every level of scale. Practice 1 · Discovery Business Scenarios The TOGAF Business Scenario technique re-expressed as a composable Essence practice. Discover and quantify the need before anything is committed. Advances → Opportunity · Stakeholders Open practice → Practice 2 · Service design Service Blueprint Visualise the end-to-end service from both the customer's and provider's perspective — frontstage, backstage, and support processes. Advances → Requirements · System Open practice → Practice 3 · Architecture Minimum Viable Architecture Architect the minimum, just in time, to retire significant risks and guide delivery. Decisions over documents — shapes the Solution alpha. Advances → Architecture · System Open practice → Practice 4 · Governance Minimum Viable Governance Govern outcomes and guardrails, not gates. Decide at the last responsible moment, as close to the work as possible. Advances → Endeavor · Way of Working Open practice → 03 How they compose The four practices stack on top of the kernel. Run them in a logical order — it is a loop, and it nests at every level of scale from enterprise to team. Practice 1 Business Scenarios Discover & quantify the need Practice 2 Service Blueprint Design the end-to-end service Practice 3 Min. Viable Architecture Architect just enough, just in time Practice 4 Min. Viable Governance Guard outcomes, not gates Foundation The Essence Kernel 7 alphas · 15 activity spaces · 6 competencies — always underneath 04 Assessment workbook EA Alpha Assessment Track alpha states across all seven kernel alphas. Sixteen tabs in three groups — tick the checklists and the dashboard moves. Documents lie; alpha states are honest. ⬇ Download workbook (.xlsx) Essence is an OMG standard developed by the SEMAT community (Ivar Jacobson and others). title: Minimum Viable Architecture — an Essence Practice
kicker: A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel
lede: Architect the minimum, just in time, to retire the significant risks and guide delivery — decisions over documents. MVA shapes the Solution; it pairs with Business Scenarios (which feeds it) and MVG (which governs it).
text: Minimum Viable Architecture — an Essence Practice Skip to main content Show All Alphas Activities Work Products Roles Patterns ⎙ Print MVA is one practice in a composed way of working. It owns the architecture-shaping elements split out from the former MVA/MVG bundle; governance now lives in the separate MVG practice. The TOGAF ADM mapping is a synthesis, not an Open Group standard.

title: Minimum Viable Governance — an Essence Practice
kicker: A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel
lede: Govern outcomes and guardrails, not gates. Decide at the last responsible moment, as close to the work as possible, and assure lightly by evidence. MVG keeps the MVA practice honest without slowing delivery.
text: Minimum Viable Governance — an Essence Practice Skip to main content Show All Alphas Activities Work Products Roles Patterns ⎙ Print MVG is the governance practice split out from the former MVA/MVG bundle; the architecture-shaping elements now live in the separate MVA practice. The TOGAF mapping is a synthesis, not an Open Group standard.

Viable Architecture Practice 4 · Governance Min. Viable Governance Essence is an OMG standard developed by the SEMAT community (Ivar Jacobson and others). This way of working composes the kernel with four practices synthesising ideas from TOGAF, service blueprinting (Shostack, 1984) and Use-Case 2.0. Keep your licensed standards as the authority.

title: Service Blueprint — an Essence Practice
kicker: A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel
lede: Visualise the service delivery process from both the customer's and the provider's perspective — customer actions, frontstage and backstage, support processes and physical evidence — across the lines of interaction, visibility and internal interactio
text: Service Blueprint — an Essence Practice Skip to main content Show All Alphas Activities Work Products Roles Patterns ⎙ Print Service blueprinting was introduced by G. Lynn Shostack (“Designing Services That Deliver”, Harvard Business Review, 1984); the layered/line structure follows common service-design practice (e.g. Nielsen Norman Group). Expressing it as an Essence practice — and the use-case analogy — is a synthesis, not an official standard. It owns one sub-alpha and advances kernel alphas the way a Use-Case 2.0 use case does.
